title: Server Updates: March Madness Edition

content:
It's been a crazy month for this server. It's also been a crazy one game for Purdue. I guess we all have our moments.

In this edition: Wireguard magic, CMS plugins, and remembering my server!

Wireguard

As about two of you remember, I moved to Oracle Cloud and used Rathole to forward traffic from ✨the cloud✨ to my server. Issue was that everything seemed to be coming from 127.0.0.1. As such, my Apache logs were filled with people attempting to go to /wp-login.php, but it seemed like the culprit was gasp me!

So, I had to rethink things. Back in the day, when I was first experimenting with all of this, I tried to use Wireguard. It did not turn out well. Nothing worked. Now, with a little more experience with Wireguard under my belt and a little more willingness to suffer through Linux networking, I got a peer-to-peer Wireguard setup working.

Now to wrangle firewalld. It doesn't seem possible to forward IP addresses over firewalld without masquerading them, at least according to this source.

So, I had to fight my worst enemy.

iptables my beloathed.

Luckily, after much pain (and downtime), I found someone else who had done all of the hard work for me. Thank you, Pro Custodibus.

The TL;DR is that I have two servers. One in the cloud that is publicly available (called proxy) and the server that actually does all of the work (called ersei.net).

This is the wg0.conf configuration on ersei.net:

[Interface]
PrivateKey = [REDACTED]
Address = 192.168.77.2/32
Table = 123

PreUp = ip rule add from 192.168.77.2 table 123 priority 456
PostDown = ip rule del from 192.168.77.2 table 123 priority 456

[Peer]
PublicKey = [REDACTED]
Endpoint = [PROXY]
AllowedIPs = 0.0.0.0/0
PersistentKeepalive = 30

and here's the wg0.conf on proxy:

[Interface]
Address = 192.168.77.1/24
ListenPort = [REDACTED]
PrivateKey = [REDACTED]

PreUp = iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.77.2:80
PreUp = iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.77.2:443

PostDown = iptables -t nat -D PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.77.2:80
PostDown = iptables -t nat -D PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.77.2:443

[Peer]
PublicKey = [ERSEI.NET]
AllowedIPs = 192.168.77.2/32
PersistentKeepalive = 30

Works perfectly. Almost like magic. I still use Rathole for services that don't need to know the connecting IP address (such as a private Minecraft server or three), but this is a much better solution for HTTP(S).

You know that feeling you get when you fix an issue that has been bothering you for a year? This was it.

Plugins Galore

I use Grav as my website's CMS. It's not particularly famous, so there isn't much of a breadth of plugins to choose from. In particular, the only plugin that rendered LaTeX is the MathJax plugin. I don't want any Javascript on my website. So, naturally, I made my own plugin. It's still pending addition to the official repository at the time of writing (2023-03-20).

The way the plugin works is that there is an HTTP server in Node.js that renders the LaTeX into the HTML and sends it back to the CMS for inclusion. It's as shrimple as that. I'm pretty proud of it too. I put together a little demo to showcase the rendering if you haven't seen it already.

Phone, Wallet, Keys… Server?

Yeah, I forgot my server at home (plugged in and running) over Winter break. Don't worry, I brought it back when I came home from spring break. Downtime should decrease now that I can much more easily fix issues that went wrong and when SSH isn't available, much like what happened when I was trying to set up Wireguard.

I still have a memory leak on my server, so I still have to figure out what's causing that and also minimize downtime. It's pretty difficult to do so, since it's intern season and I should probably have my personal website available for people to see if they wanna check out my work. That is, if they even read through my résumé.

Finally, it seems like I had accidentally disabled turbo boost on my server. It's back on now. Everything is a lot faster. Read through that Mastodon thread to really get a feel for my emotions at the time.

Thoughts? Comments? Opinions? Feel free to share (relevant) ones with me! Contact me here if you want.
